data.title: AAR: Firing On All Cylinders
data.description:
In August, I concluded that shares of AAR (AIR) were cruising to fresh highs. The company was delivering strong revenue and earnings growth following the integration of the acquisition of Triumph.

With elevated leverage rapidly coming down, there are some drivers for continued earnings growth in 2026, and even as shares were trading near their all-time highs, more momentum was seen.

Momentum and news flow quickly arrived in the fall, after AAR announced no less than 3 deals while offering some equity to keep leverage in check as well. Such moves and continued operating momentum make me happy to maintain a position, even at current higher levels.

Aviation Aftermarket Services

AAR provides aviation aftermarket services, with the business founded soon after World War II. Such products and services include parts, integrated solutions, expeditionary services, and repair & engineering services.

Positioned as an independent provider of such services, the company employs over 6,000 workers who provide such services and products across the globe. AAR serves commercial carriers as well as defense and governmental agencies, although the commercial market makes up about two-thirds of sales.

By now the company generates about $2.7 billion in sales, with parts supply being the largest segment, making up about 40% of sales. This is largely complemented by repair & engineering and integrated solutions, with expeditionary services having become a minimal unit with the passage of time. The repair & engineering units have seen a considerable bump following a $725 million deal to acquire Triumph, a transaction announced late in 2023.

The company is operating in a massive, nearly $140 billion global MRO market, which provides continued growth and consolidation opportunities, coming on top of solid growth achievements already delivered. Over the past decade, the company has nearly doubled sales, with these sales obviously taking a dip during the pandemic. Operating margins have risen from mid-single digits to high single digits, all amidst a pretty stable share count.

About The Valuation

In July of this year, AAR reported a 20% increase in fiscal 2025 sales to $2.78 billion, as EBITDA was up 34% to $324 million. Net debt was reported at $880 million, for a leverage ratio reported at 2.7 times. All this allowed for adjusted earnings of $3.91 per share.

While no formal 2026 guidance was issued, the company struck an upbeat tone, as the company announced a $15 million deal to acquire Aerostrat in August, adding long-range maintenance planning software, as this was truly a bolt-on transaction.

Trading at $77 in August, shares traded at 19 times trailing earnings, which were seen advancing further in 2026, certainly as the company has been incurring substantial interest expenses on its debt load. This could allow for real deleveraging amidst debt refinancing and debt reduction. I was working with a $4.25-$4.50 per share earnings number in the fiscal year 2026. With leverage seen in the low 2s, and earnings multiples seen at 17-18 times, shares looked fairly valued, with potential for shares to continue to fly higher going forward.

Eventful Times

Since August, it has been busy on the corporate front for AAR. In September, the company reported a solid 12% increase in first-quarter sales to $740 million. Margins kept improving, with EBITDA up 18% to $87 million, as adjusted earnings rose some 27% to $1.08 per share. Net debt actually ticked up to $942 million, yet with EBITDA trending around $337 million, that was not a concern, as the strong start made me feel really comfortable with the earnings numbers mentioned above.

Days later, AAR announced the acquisition of American Distributors Holding, a distributor of components and assemblies, in a $146 million cash deal. Founded in 1983, ADI employs about 400 workers, who generate $149 million in sales and $15 million in EBITDA, revealing that a 1 times sales multiple and nearly 10 times EBITDA multiple have been paid.

With pro forma net debt seen up to $1.09 billion, the company sold 3 million shares towards the end of the month. At an offer price of $83 per share, this yields a quarter of a billion in gross proceeds, for pro forma net debt around $850 million. Note that some dilution will be incurred, with the transaction seen increasing the count from about 36 million shares to 39 million shares, yet it allows for the purchase of ADI and net deleveraging at the same time.

Aided by this influx, AAR announced the purchase of HAECO Americas in a $78 million cash deal, adding to the repair & engineering segment, although no further financial details have been announced. Just before the year end, AAR announced a $35 million cash deal to acquire Aircraft Reconfig Technologies to expand engineering and certification capabilities, with again no financial details being announced.

In between the three deals and the share issuance, I peg the net debt load at just over $950 million, as I see leverage ratios in the mid-2s. The company has incurred about 8% dilution here, but the ADI deal alone will boost sales by about 5%, with an unknown revenue contribution from the most recent two deals.

And Now?

Following an acquisitive period, which seems to have been paid for with higher-valued shares, I think that AAR is doing just fine here. Shares have seen further gains, now trading at $85 per share, trading within imminent sight of the highs around $90 per share.

Following the strong start to the year and the M&A moves over the fall, I feel very comfortable working with a $4.25-$4.50 per share estimate for the year, potentially seeing upside to that. This now pushes up forward earnings multiples to 19-20 times earnings, with upside to positive delivery. For all that, I anxiously look forward to learning more about the second quarter earnings report.

Hence, I continue to see AAR operating from a position of strength and applaud this. Having held a small position, I am backing the recent moves made, while the business itself does just fine, making me a patient holder of the stock.

data.title: Immatics: PRAME Leader's Transition To Commercial Reality
data.description:
My optimistic view on Immatics (IMTX) is driven by the progress of the company's three-wave strategy. What we are seeing is the execution of a systematic approach to commercializing the company's products: Wave one establishes a foothold in the treatment of melanoma with anzu-cel; Wave two expands the company's presence into other types of cancers that are difficult to treat, such as ovarian cancer, using the next generation of cell therapy products called IMA203CD8; and Wave three makes TCR therapeutics available to all patients by introducing "off-the-shelf" bispecific products such as IMA402. I believe that the fact that Immatics is currently enrolling patients in a Phase 3 trial and has sufficient cash reserves to last until the company launches commercially provides significant risk reduction relative to its peer group and offers a compelling investment opportunity for investors who are willing to look beyond the short-term volatility associated with investing in biotech companies.

Current Financial Health

As of September 30, 2025, Immatics has a healthy amount of cash on hand; a sum of $505.8 million of cash, cash equivalents, and other financial instruments. Based upon the company’s guidance, this cash will allow Immatics to continue operating into the second half of 2027. This timeframe is important because it encompasses both the Biologics License Application submission scheduled for the first half of 2027 and the subsequent commercial launch later in the same year. Therefore, the company will have sufficient funds available throughout its initial commercial milestone.

An analysis of the income statement demonstrates that total revenue for the quarter was $6.1 million. At first glance, this appears to be a dramatic decline in comparison to the $59.4 million of revenue that Immatics recorded in the same quarter of 2024. Context, however, is essential to understanding the data presented above. The previous year’s revenue included a non-recurring revenue recognition event resulting from the termination of the IMA401 collaboration between Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY) and Immatics. The revenue generated in Q3 2025 reflects a normalized rate of growth from the ongoing but much smaller collaborations with which Immatics is currently involved. While gaining back full rights to IMA401 could potentially add value to the company’s business model, as it would give Immatics the opportunity to make decisions regarding the combinations of their bispecific products, the decline in revenue is clearly not indicative of a weakness in the business model.

On the expense side of the balance sheet, R&D spending increased to $55.4 million as compared to $45.7 million in the same quarter of the prior year. The company is using the large amounts of capital it has raised to advance its clinical trials, including the SUPRAME Phase 3 trial and the expansion of the bispecific cohorts. G&A expenses were modestly higher at $14.9 million due to the natural increase in pre-commercial spending that occurs as companies begin to prepare for the launch of new products. The reported net loss of $59.3 million for the quarter is consistent with the level of spending that the company has incurred in terms of its operational tempo. With the existing burn rate and the sizeable amount of cash available to fund operations, Immatics is able to create a financial safety net that enables the company to engage in negotiations with potential partners or financiers without appearing to be desperate.

PRAME and Clinical Pipeline

The core of the Immatics investment thesis is focused on its PRAME franchise and is unfolding in three stages. The first stage will be driven by the launch of anzutresgene autoleucel, or anzu-cel (formerly IMA203), an autologous TCR-T cell therapy targeted at patients suffering from metastatic melanoma. The Phase 3 SUPRAME trial is now underway where anzu-cel will be compared to investigator’s choice in patients with unresectable or metastatic cutaneous melanoma who have previously been treated with checkpoint inhibitors. This trial will provide the required data to achieve the company’s first regulatory approval.

The second wave of products in the pipeline includes IMA203CD8, a second generation of cell therapy. In contrast to anzu-cel, IMA203CD8 involves the co-transduction of CD8 along with the PRAME TCR. As a result, CD4+ T cells can assist in the attack on the tumor by recruiting them to the site. By using two different types of T cells instead of one, the expectation is that the efficacy of the product will be increased as well as the duration of the response. Historically, solid tumors have proven to be less responsive to immunotherapies than melanoma, and therefore this is expected to enable Immatics to expand its target population beyond melanoma to include a broad spectrum of tumor types, including gynecological cancers such as ovarian and uterine carcinoma.

The third wave of products includes the TCR Bispecifics, specifically IMA402 and IMA401. Similar to monoclonal antibodies, these are off-the-shelf biologics that function to bind to both a patient’s T cells and the tumor cells, thereby acting as a “glue.” Compared to autologous cell therapies, there is a significant advantage to this approach. In order to produce an autologous cell therapy, each patient requires the production of a separate product. In contrast, a biologic can be produced in large quantities and stored in freezers. Once thawed, the biologic is ready for administration. Recent clinical results have demonstrated that Immatics is capable of translating their TCR experience to a bi-specific format, while maintaining the affinity and specificity of the TCR and minimizing the toxicity issues that commonly occur when using bi-specific agents.

Recent Clinical Updates

Anzu-cel has a validated Phase 1b treatment for metastatic uveal melanoma based upon the updated data that were presented at ESMO 2025. Of particular note is the fact that anzu-cel had a confirmed objective response rate of 67% (of 16 patients), which is a notable achievement since it has been proven to be very difficult to treat metastatic uveal melanoma; additionally, the response rate was generated in a population that was heavily pretreated. The median duration of response was 11.0 months, and although the median overall survival had not been reached at a median follow-up of 14.3 months, it suggests that anzu-cel provides durable benefits as opposed to merely transient tumor shrinkage. Therefore, the data supporting the potential of anzu-cel led to the start of a dedicated Phase 2 cohort for uveal melanoma; the data also demonstrates management's commitment to this specific indication.

Although the data supporting the potential of anzu-cel are clearly important for the long-term growth story of the company, perhaps even more important for the long-term growth story is the update announced by the company on December 11, 2025, regarding IMA203CD8. One of the primary concerns with TCR-T therapies is that they may not function well in cancers outside of those that are highly immunogenic, such as melanoma. However, the data presented at the ESMO Immuno-Oncology Congress 2025 clearly address this concern. The data support the idea that IMA203CD8 is functioning in ovarian carcinoma, a tumor type that historically has been difficult to treat with immunotherapy. At doses of 2.3 billion cells or higher, the company reported 2 confirmed partial responses and 1 ongoing metabolic complete response among 5 patients.

The safety profile of IMA203CD8 remains manageable; specifically, there were no grade 5 adverse events associated with the therapy; the company is currently investigating protocols that would eliminate the need for post-infusion low-dose IL-2. If the need for post-infusion low-dose IL-2 could be eliminated, it would likely improve the patient experience and safety profile of the therapy, thus improving the attractiveness of the therapy to practicing oncologists in the community setting.

Counterarguments and Risks

For Immatics, the major challenge to its ability to generate revenue from the sale of an autologous cell therapy (anzu-cel) will be the logistics involved in making it commercially viable. In contrast to selling a pill or a vial of drug product, anzu-cel involves collecting the patient’s cells; transporting the collected cells to a manufacturing facility where they are genetically modified; and then returning the genetically modified cells to the patient. Although Immatics has built a dedicated 100,000 square foot manufacturing facility in Houston to facilitate this process, there is still a significant operational execution risk associated with manufacturing and vein-to-vein delivery times. Therefore, if either manufacturing or vein-to-vein delivery times are delayed, then the ability to successfully launch anzu-cel in the marketplace may be limited by those delays regardless of the efficacy of the drug.

SUPRAME Phase 3 is an event-driven study; therefore, the timeline for completion of the data analysis of the trial results will depend upon how quickly patients experience disease progression or die. Therefore, while Immatics anticipates completing the first interim analysis and final analysis in 2026, biological variability makes it difficult to predict when the actual date of the first interim analysis and/or final analysis will occur. If the actual date of the first interim analysis and/or final analysis is later than expected, then the timing of the BLA filing and cash flow generation will be later than anticipated, which may require a capital raise prior to the end of 2027.

Addressable Market and Competitive Landscape

In the U.S. and the EU5 countries (France, Germany, Italy, Spain, United Kingdom), there are approximately 9,000 patients per year that suffer from PRAME-positive, HLA-A*02:01 positive cutaneous and uveal melanoma. Although this is a niche market, it is a high value niche and sets up the commercial infrastructure.

As we grow beyond this first market, the next step is with IMA203CD8, targeting gynecological cancers and other solid tumors; the number of patients addressed grows dramatically. The company believes that when all indications (such as ovarian, uterine, and non-small cell lung cancer) are included in the cell therapy franchise, the number of patients addressed in the U.S. and EU5 would be in excess of 75,000 per year. At this point, the revenue potential moves from hundreds of millions to billions.

The bispecifics provide the greatest breadth of application. Because they can be used in the earlier lines of therapy and potentially in conjunction with checkpoint inhibitors, IMA402 provides access to a patient population of over 145,000 patients per year in the same geographies. Additionally, the combination of IMA401 (MAGEA4/8) and IMA402 (PRAME) in squamous non-small cell lung cancer offers a significant opportunity. Data indicates that over 90% of sqNSCLC patients are positive for either PRAME or MAGEA4/8, and approximately 60% of patients are positive for both of these antigens. This dual-targeting approach may provide access to a patient population of approximately 40,000 patients per year, and represents a comprehensive solution for this difficult-to-treat indication.

Competitively, while companies such as Immunocore (IMCR) have a commercial product (tebentafusp) for uveal melanoma, Immatics' dominance in the PRAME space is unique. Tebentafusp is limited to gp100-positive patients, and has a different mechanism than anzu-cel. Immatics' anzu-cel has demonstrated efficacy in patients who have progressed after treatment with tebentafusp, indicating non-overlapping utility. Immatics is the most advanced in the PRAME space with the broadest portfolio, establishing a defensive moat around this high-value target.

Future Outlook and Conclusion

While there are risks associated with manufacturing logistics and competition, Immatics has taken steps to address these issues through its investment in its Houston facility, and the unique characteristics of its T-cell receptor bispecific drugs, which provide a differentiator from competing treatments. Therefore, for investors who wish to participate in the next generation of breakthroughs in immuno-oncology, and who require both near-term commercialization visibility and long-term optionality in their portfolio, Immatics provides a viable alternative. As such, the data indicates that Immatics' vision to have a positive, life-changing impact on cancer patients is rapidly being realized, and it is reasonable to believe that Immatics' valuation will reflect this maturation as the company moves toward its 2027 product launch.

data.title: Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending: Trades At A Deep Discount For A Reason
data.description:
Overview

Business development companies were one of the best sectors to collect a high dividend yield from only a few years ago. As the interest rate environment remains elevated, BDCs have struggled to grow their portfolios and earnings as portfolio companies feel the burden of a higher cost of debt. Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending Corp. (NCDL) is a BDC I haven't covered before, so I wanted to take some time to assess its value proposition as we approach the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NCDL's share price has declined by about 18.15%. So as the debt market continues to face headwinds, NCDL has struggled to regain positive growth momentum. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of 7.92% over the same time frame. Following the declines, NCDL now offers a very generous dividend yield of 13%. However, I remain very cautious about the sustainability of the distributions based on the latest earnings results. It appears that the portfolio is struggling to generate earnings that exceed the distribution amounts.

NCDL now trades at one of its deepest discounts to NAV valuations ever, but I believe this is a result of the underlying challenges that it faces. The portfolio has failed to grow its earnings, and management struggles to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity. As a result, the underlying net asset value continues to erode quarter after quarter. Therefore, I remain cautious about entry here and believe that investors should remain on the sidelines until momentum starts to shift. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that NCDL implements within its portfolio.

Q3 Earnings

Unlike traditional equities, NCDL generates its earnings by investing in a diverse pool of debt investments. NCDL essentially lends capital to borrowers and collects interest or equity in exchange. So to succeed, NCDL is relying on a borrower's ability to keep up with its required debt maintenance. According to the latest portfolio breakdown, NCDL has investments at a total fair value of $1.96B that are spread across 213 different portfolio companies. In order to reduce risks, NCDL structures the majority of its investments as first lien debt, which sits at the top of the corporate capital structure and has the highest priority for repayment. The portfolio's structure by investment type is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 89.8%
  • Subordinated Debt: 8.1%
  • Equity: 2.1%

Expanding on this diversity, we can see that NCDL's portfolio also spans across a diverse range of industries to mitigate any sort of concentration risks. According to the latest breakdown, NCDL is most heavily allocated to Healthcare & Pharmaceutical investments at 17.6% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Services: Business and Beverage, Food, & Tobacco investments, which account for 16.5% and 7.6% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three industries makes up more than 41% of total assets, so investors should anticipate that these areas will have the greatest impact on NCDL's total return potential.

NCDL reported its Q3 earnings in early November, and the results were mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.43 per share, which is equivalent to $21.3M during the quarter. This was a sizeable decline from the prior year's net investment income of $0.58 per share. Additionally, NCDL experienced a net realized loss of $0.05 per share for the quarter, which resulted in a total net increase in net operations of $0.38 per share.

It appears that the main contributor to the lower earnings is the decline in interest income that NCDL has experienced. For instance, the amount of interest income received from portfolio companies amounted to $48.2M, which was a large decline from the prior year's interest income of $57.3M. Total investment income, including dividend, PIK interest, and other income, amounted to $51M, which was also a large decline from the prior year's total investment income of $60.2M.

As earnings decline, this is one of the clearest indications that the portfolio companies within the portfolio may be struggling to keep up with their debt payments. Additionally, it could be a sign that the selected portfolio companies are struggling to thrive under the higher interest rate environment, since 94.2% of the borrowers are structured on a floating rate basis. A higher interest rate means that portfolio companies will need to pay out a larger portion of their earnings towards servicing debt payments.

In more positive news, management has been able to reduce the internal operating expenses over the last quarter to help slow the negative impact of earnings. For instance, total expenses amounted to $29.7M during the quarter, which was a decline from the prior year's total operating expenses of $34.2M. The lower expenses can be attributed to the reduction in interest expenses and professional fees. However, management fees did considerably increase year-over-year, now totaling $5.1M.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since NCDL is structured as a BDC, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, NCDL now trades at a large discount to NAV of 22.46%. For reference, NCDL has traded at an average discount to NAV of 11.83% over the last twelve months. While this larger discount may seem like an attractive opportunity to add, I'd argue that this is a direct reflection of the challenges that NCDL is currently facing. Therefore, I do not think this is a great time to accumulate.

A big source of the troubles comes from NCDL's failure to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity. For instance, NCDL experienced new investment funding of $29.2M during the latest quarter. However, this was offset by sales and repayments of $61.3M, which ultimately resulted in a net decrease in investment activity of $25.04M during the quarter. When you combine the lack of growth with the limited earnings growth, we can see that NCDL's NAV continues to decline. For reference, here are the quarter-end NAV amounts:

  • Q3 2024: $18.15 per share
  • Q4 2024: $18.18 per share
  • Q1 2025: $17.96 per share
  • Q2 2025: $17.92 per share
  • Q3 2025: $17.85 per share

As long as interest rates remain elevated, I believe that management will struggle to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment activity. The higher cost of debt was also a reason why NCDL experienced two new non-accruals during the quarter. Non-accruals represent the rate of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and struggling to keep up with their debt payments. According to the latest earnings call, non-accruals still remain relatively low compared to many other BDC peers.

As of September 30, nonaccruals represent just 0.4% of our total investment portfolio on a fair value basis and 0.9% on a cost basis. We believe the strength of our platform, including experienced workout and portfolio management teams will continue to drive favorable results. Portfolio diversification remains a key focus of ours within our overall investment portfolio. - Kenneth Kencel, CEO

As non-accruals increase, the overall risk profile of a BDC increases because this indicates that the underlying investments are vulnerable to current market conditions. A higher non-accrual rate means that net investment income is negatively impacted. To be fair though, many other BDCs are facing similar challenges as they see their non-accrual rate rise. When compared to peers, NCDL's non-accruals aren't that bad. For reference, here are some non-accrual rates for peer BDCs:

  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): 1.3% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Main Street Capital (MAIN): 1.2% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Questionable Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.45 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13%. As previously mentioned, net investment income for the quarter amounted to $0.43 per share, which is a clear indication that earnings no longer support the distributions being paid. As long as NCDL pays out more than it actually earns in income, the underlying NAV will decline, growth will remain limited, and the dividend will remain at the risk of a cut in the near-term. However, management reassured investors by stating that they feel confident in their ability to continue supporting the current dividend level.

We felt good about our ability to continue to earn, again, within $0.01 or $0.02. Obviously, we under-earned by $0.02 this quarter our dividend of $0.45. But again, within a range, and there were some reasons for that, including the two nonaccrual names that I alluded to in terms of reducing our earnings for this particular period. - Kenneth Kencel, CEO

Despite management's reassurance, I believe that keeping the dividend unchanged may be a mistake. By continuously paying out more than it generates in income, the NAV will continue to deteriorate if management isn't able to offset this with new investment activity. I fear that NCDL may be kicking the can down the road and may end up cutting the dividend in the future when the damage is already done. Management did confirm that there is spillover income available, but didn't specifically cover how large of a bucket this remains.

Additionally, investors should consider the fact that the distributions received from NCDL do not offer any tax efficiency. Since payouts are supported by interest income collected from borrowers, the distributions would be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in NCDL would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors looking to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that NCDL is a sell at this time due to the declining NAV, poor new investment activity, and failure to support distributions. Although NCDL's portfolio maintains an efficient level of diversity across different industries, the underlying earnings are weak. The portfolio still has a low rate of non-accruals, but the growth metrics are underwhelming. Unless management allocates a greater amount of capital towards new investments, I believe that the NAV will continue to trend downward unless the dividend is cut. Therefore, I believe that the discounted price to NAV valuation is a direct reflection of the headwinds faced, rather than an attractive opportunity to accumulate.

data.title: Aehr Test Systems: AI Processing Opportunity Looks Interesting, But Don't Be In A Rush
data.description:
Introduction

Aehr Test Systems, Inc. (AEHR) is a California-based supplier of semiconductor test and burn-in equipment that is used by semi manufacturers primarily to ascertain and mark flawed chips at the wafer level before they are packed. It wouldn’t be a stretch to suggest that AEHR is something of an authority in this stress-testing space, having honed its burn-in testing expertise for over four-and-a-half decades.

It also appears that 2025 as a whole has been very rewarding for AEHR’s shareholder base; to elaborate, on a YTD basis, the AEHR stock has expanded by close to 36%, marginally outperforming other stocks from the semiconductor industry (on average) and generating almost three times the returns of its peers from the Russell 2000. Having said that, do also consider that since October the tide appears to have turned, with AEHR giving away some of its previously sizeable gains (it was up by over 80% for the first nine months of 2025).

So, should investors buy into this intermediate weakness, or can they afford to be patient?

Business Slowdown to Persist for a Couple More Quarters

Basically, investors who want to pursue AEHR should first consider that this is not a business that attracts a wide portfolio of high-volume clients, and thus, one tends to see some lumpiness across quarters; although the situation has improved from what it was a couple of years back (97% of revenue came from its top 5 clients back then), AEHR’s 5 largest clients still contribute over 75% of its revenue, and, too, cancellations and deferrals are not uncommon, given limited penalties.

This year, especially in the second half of the calendar year (although note that AEHR subscribes to a May-ending fiscal), tariff announcements have triggered some uncertainties at AEHR’s customers’ end (tariffs are also impacting the imported parts of AEHR and leaving an adverse mark on gross margins, which we’ll touch on later), and that has prevented AEHR management from providing formal guidance for the FY (year ending May 2026).

Given what we’ve written in the previous paragraph, investors should not be surprised to see that AEHR has seen quite a wobbly revenue trajectory in 2025. To elaborate, for the February-ending quarter (Q3-25), YoY revenue growth was up by an impressive 140% YoY, but in the following two quarters, revenue has slumped, with the most recent decline coming in at the -16% level.

Take note of the forward-looking backlog as well (which typically gets converted over a time frame of 12 months); sequentially, it has barely grown at around $15.5M, and on a YoY basis, it is down by -7%.

Unsurprisingly, given the tepid backlog growth, consensus suggests that AEHR revenue weakness will continue for the next couple of quarters at the very least, before growth returns in Q4-26 (the May 2026 quarter) as the currently soft Power Silicon Carbide (SiC) market (keenly linked to BEV and automotive appetite coming back, as this segment accounts for 70% of demand) is expected to start displaying some resilience by then.

Investors should also consider that the slowdown in volumes also leaves an unfavorable mark on the company’s gross margins [GM] due to underutilization of their manufacturing capacity (higher import costs and an unfavorable product mix are also playing a part in GM weakness). Note that GMs have taken quite a brutal hit over the last two quarters (down by roughly 1700bps YoY on average) and are currently at sub-40% levels.

Until volumes come back, don’t expect a meaningful uplift in margins either, although it’s fair to say they have probably bottomed out as well.

Well Positioned to Service High-Power AI Processors

Amidst these broader pressures, AEHR should not be written off because of its rising capabilities in servicing the AI processor market (the semiconductor category with the greatest growth potential through this decade), which has only been enhanced following last year’s acquisition of Incal Technology, a specialist in ultra-high-power solutions.

Offering testing and burn-in systems at the wafer level has been AEHR’s core competence, but by getting Incal on board, AEHR is now the “only company worldwide” that can also offer “packaged part” burn-in systems for high-power AI processors (besides the wafer-level expertise).

With the influx of multiple processors and memory stacks, overall power requirements go through the roof (AI GPUs can typically consume close to 1000 MW of power) and also require liquid-cooled conductive temperature control to manage the high thermal levels.

AEHR’s flagship product for this space, the Sonoma brand of burn-in systems, is not just seeing traction in new orders but also follow-on orders from hyperscalers and data center providers that are in the process of introducing new AI processors that need to be tested (collaborations are also on for future generations of processors). It’s worth noting that in the most recent quarter, the company also conducted a customer open house highlighting these Sonoma systems, with 10 different companies showing an interest in the same.

AEHR is not just resting on its laurels and has been enhancing the capabilities of Sonoma, with the newest version coming across as fully automated and increasing power per device to 2000 watts!

AEHR’s Forward Valuations Look Concerning

Last year AEHR wasn’t profitable from a GAAP perspective and ended up reporting a net loss per share of -$0.13. This year, they are expected to get back in the black, but consensus expects an EPS of just $0.06. In light of the low EPS base, AEHR’s price of $22.61 looks quite concerning, as it translates to an exorbitant multiple of 376x. To put that multiple into further context, note that it represents a 9.4x premium over other semiconductor equipment and material stocks, which can be picked up at an average forward earnings multiple of 40x.

Technicals - Loss of Momentum, With a Build-up in Shorts, Although 200dma Continues to Be Defended

When we view AEHR’s charts, we feel neutral positioning would be the right call at this juncture.

The image below highlights AEHR’s daily price movements, and what we can see is that the period of higher-highs [HHs] and higher-lows [HLs] that was evident from May to October has reversed. In November, we also saw the stock break down from its ascending trendline, and despite attempts to recoup the trendline this month, we are yet to see that transpire (reflecting a lack of bullish conviction). However, what’s encouraging to see is that AEHR has not yet given up on defending its landmark 200DMA.

Having said that, the bulls of AEHR should also be mindful of a growing cohort of short-sellers since November; after showing meaningful declines since the turn of 2025, the short-interest has grown by around 14% since November, and at 5.1M, now accounts for almost 20% of AEHR’s total float.

AEHR’s positioning vs the broad semiconductor universe should also be taken into account; earlier in Q1 of this year, AEHR may have come across as an intriguing upward mean reversion bet within this universe, as the stock’s relative strength ratio vs its peers was trading at a discount to its long-term mean of 0.057. That trade has since played out, with the current Relative Strength now perched at a 20% premium over the long-term mean.

Summary

The AEHR stock, which has experienced a commendable 2025 overall, appears to be facing some intermediate roadblocks as we close the year, driven by uncertainties at the customers’ end and underutilization of AEHR’s resources, which are also impacting margins. Management’s limited visibility and further potential top line weakness over the next couple of quarters suggest that investors don’t necessarily have to be in a mad rush to buy into this weakness, particularly when the stock is priced at an exorbitant forward earnings multiple of 376x (almost 10x more than the industry average).

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Massively Expanding Its Turf
data.description:
As we prepare for 2026, choppy stock markets require active portfolio management for next year. On the whole, I am quite skeptical of large-cap tech and growth stocks after a massive run-up this year, and I think we're overdue for a category rotation in favor of small- and mid-cap stocks. That said, when we look beyond the "obvious" AI plays that have driven the market's gains this year, I still find several large-cap names to be attractive opportunities.

Palo Alto Networks (PANW) is one of these stocks. Now unquestionably one of the largest pure-play cybersecurity stocks, Palo Alto Networks has seen its share price bump up only ~5% this year, as investors have expressed skepticism over the company's empire-building via M&A. In my view, however, I think Palo Alto Networks trades at reasonable levels against a substantially expanded TAM.

I last wrote a buy article on Palo Alto Networks in September, when the stock was hovering above $200 per share. Since then, the stock has slid lower, especially on news of the company's deal to acquire Chronosphere. Still, I like the fact that the company is marching toward an aggressive FCF target, amid strong organic execution on its own. I'm reiterating my buy rating here.

First things first, we'll discuss Chronosphere and its impact on Palo Alto Networks' financials. In November, the company acquired Chronosphere for $3.35 billion in cash and stock. The company expects the deal to close in the second half of FY26, around the same time as the closing of CyberArk.

Chronosphere is an observability platform. Palo Alto Networks' stance is that the AI era is necessitating enormous amounts of data consumption, which necessitates solutions like Chronosphere to ensure that infrastructure systems are always-on and operating at full capacity. The company posits that many existing observability platforms aren't built to scale alongside the enormous data consumption required for AI infrastructure, which is the exact problem that Chronosphere aims to solve: providing scalable observability services at a reasonable total cost at scale.

To me, the economics of this deal are even more favorable than when Palo Alto Networks acquired CyberArk (at a mid-teens multiple of revenue for ~40% growth). The company notes that Chronosphere has $160 million in ARR, growing at a triple-digit y/y pace. If this pace of growth sustains into next year, this could mean that Palo Alto Networks is paying just a ~10x acquisition multiple (~$3.35 billion for a company that can hit >$300 million in ARR quickly) to enter into a brand-new market with no overlap with existing cybersecurity products. We note that this puts Palo Alto Networks head-to-head with observability/infrastructure monitoring leader Datadog (DDOG), at the exact same time that the latter is starting to expand into more security-driven use cases.

The chart below, meanwhile, showcases how Palo Alto Networks' TAM has evolved in the past several years alone. Adding brand new markets in observability (Chronosphere) and identity governance (CyberArk) now positions Palo Alto Networks' long-term TAM at a whopping $300 billion, against which the company's current ~$10 billion revenue clip is only ~3% penetrated.

We like that Palo Alto Networks is using its rich share valuation and healthy cash flow as a currency to keep bolstering its growth potential. On top of strong organic execution, I think Palo Alto Networks is poised to thrive in 2026 after a year of underperformance against the S&P 500.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Palo Alto Networks' latest Q1 (October quarter) results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Palo Alto Networks' revenue grew 16% y/y to $2.47 billion, ahead of Wall Street's expectations of $2.46 billion (+15% y/y) by a one-point margin. Notable is the fact that revenue growth didn't decelerate at all from Q4's growth pace, while also exceeding the company's internal 14-15% guidance for the quarter.

What I continue to find as one of the most convincing indicators of Palo Alto's long term success is its continued buildup in remaining performance obligations (RPO), or essentially backlog. RPO grew 24% y/y to $15.5 billion, which is a helpful metric that suggests we aren't likely to see any near-term revenue deceleration.

We note that acquisitions are likely to further bolster Palo Alto Networks' revenue. CyberArk continued to report standalone Q3 (September quarter) results that showcased 43% y/y revenue growth and even faster 45% y/y ARR growth, as shown below:

And as previously mentioned, though not exactly disclosed because it's a private company, Chromosphere is reported to grow ARR at a triple-digit y/y pace. Together, these two acquired pieces will represent ~20% of the Palo Alto Networks' consolidated ARR (versus ~$5.8 billion today), though that 20% is growing at a much faster pace - lifting Palo Alto Networks' organic growth rates post-close.

The addition of multiple new products spanning new markets is a perfect fit for the company's "platformization" strategy. The company now has 1450 customers on platform deals, up 32% y/y and added 60 net-new platform deals in the quarter. Management notes that customers are preferring to consolidate under one banner as opposed to "vendor sprawl," which is benefiting multi-product platforms like Palo Alto Networks. Network security and firewalls continue to be Palo Alto Networks' "anchor" product, but it now has a vast opportunity to cross-sell into new areas.

Per CEO Nikesh Arora's remarks on the Q1 earnings call:

In Q1, platformization once again drove large deals across multiple industry verticals. This included U.S. federal, where we had a strong quarter and notable competitive wins. One example was a $33 million SASE deal with a U.S. cabinet agency securing 60,000 seats. This agency displaced a major SASE incumbent as they needed a platform to provide unified visibility across both their firewall estate and remote endpoints.

Another example was a $100 million deal with a large U.S. telecom provider. This included an $85 million commitment to XSIAM, which is our largest XSIAM deal ever. This customer chose us to consolidate their disparate point products based on the ability of our platform to deliver materially faster mean time to respond.

The common theme across these large transactions is clear. Customers are moving from managing vendor sprawl to demanding superior demonstrable security outcomes through platformization. The natural place for customers to start their journey is network security, which remains our largest business."

Palo Alto Networks has also continued to achieve economies of scale, with pro forma operating margins in Q1 hitting 30.2%, rising 140 bps y/y.

Palo Alto Networks remains a staunch "Rule of 40" software company. And unlike many M&A deals, we expect CyberArk not to be dilutive, as CyberARk currently enjoys ~20% operating margins on top of >40% revenue growth (a "Rule of 60" company). We note as well that the company has reaffirmed its commitment to hiring a 40%+ FCF margin by FY28, inclusive of all acquisitions.

Valuation and key takeaways

At current share prices near $187, Palo Alto Networks trades at a market cap of $130.49 billion. After we net off the $10.19 billion of cash on the company's most recent balance sheet, the stock's resulting enterprise value is $120.30 billion.

For FY26 (the year ending in July 2026), Palo Alto Networks has guided to $10.50-$1.54 billion in revenue (+14% y/y), which is an improvement versus a previous range of $10.475-$10.525 billion. It's also now expecting a ~1-point jump in pro forma operating margins to 29.5-30.0% (29.2-29.7% previously), and a 38.5% midpoint FCF margin that implies $4.05 billion in midpoint FCF. We note that all of these guidance figures exclude the impact of M&A, as closing times are not yet firm (though the company's balance sheet and share counts are also at pre-deal levels, making it a clean comparison).

On a standalone basis, Palo Alto Networks trades at:

  • 11.4x EV/FY26 revenue
  • 29.7x EV/FY26 FCF

No, the stock is not a value play. At the same time, I find a ~30x FCF multiple to be quite appealing for a company that is organically growing backlog at a >20% pace despite already sitting at a >$10 billion annualized revenue run rate, and has made the right moves to multiply its long-term TAM to >$300 billion while still affirming FCF growth targets.

Stay long here: in my view, there are plenty of catalysts to drive further upside for Palo Alto Networks.

data.title: EVG: Attractively Valued And Reliable Monthly Dividend
data.description:
Overview

Market indices continue to hover near their all-time highs, despite concerns of overvaluation. While equities expose investors to a lot of uncertainty, there are plenty of income-focused funds that offer an attractive value proposition. For instance, the Eaton Vance Short Duration Diversified Income Fund (EVG) operates as a closed-end fund that provides diverse exposure to income-producing securities. The fund provides some global exposure and has a solid historical track record of solid returns and income generation. In a scenario where interest rates continue to decline over the next twelve months, I believe that EVG is aligned for success.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that EVG's share price has declined by about 1.2%. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to nearly 7% over the same time frame. EVG now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 8.3% and issues payouts on a monthly basis. Additionally, EVG has an excellent track record at keeping payouts in a tight range, since the underlying portfolio strategy produces earnings that can support distributions. Therefore, EVG is a solid option for investors seeking a reliable source of dividend income.

Additionally, EVG trades at an attractive price-to-NAV level that may justify the accumulation of shares. The current discount may be attributed to the higher interest rate environment and the fund's use of leverage. However, long-term investors may be able to use this to their advantage by accumulating while shares remain suppressed. There's a possibility that future interest rate cuts can help EVG regain some positive price momentum. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that EVG implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, EVG has total managed assets of $1192.2M that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. EVG has a listed expense ratio of 3.07%, but this is misleading because it includes fees related to interest and other operating costs. When excluding these other costs, the fund has an actual management fee of 1.17% of assets. EVG has the primary objective of providing a high current income while providing some global exposure. The portfolio's breakdown by region is large, but the top ten regions are as follows:

  • United States: 76.5%
  • Canada: 2.20%
  • Romania: 1.10%
  • Ukraine: 1%
  • Mexico: 0.9%
  • Venezuela: 0.8%
  • Vietnam: 0.7%
  • Dominican Republic: 0.6%
  • Egypt: 0.6%
  • Tanzania: 0.6%

Expanding on this diversity, the fund also utilizes a wide range of different securities to help get exposure to different sectors. For instance, EVG is most heavily allocated to mortgage-backed securities at 31.4% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to domestic senior loans and foreign obligations, which accounts for 21.3% and 18% of assets, respectively. Although it isn't specifically highlighted in any of the reporting documents, we can assume that a portion of EVG's investments are structured on a floating rate basis. This is a very common structure for debt investments and helps the fund collect a higher level of interest income during periods of higher interest rates.

As the fund's name implies, EVG also implements a low-duration strategy. This means that EVG limits its portfolio to securities that have maturities that are no longer than three years out. The idea here is that this allows the portfolio to remain more resilient to shifting interest rate cycles. As interest rates rise, the value of longer-duration securities tends to fall, so the focus on shorter-duration securities helps the fund remain a bit more resilient.

Additionally, I like the fact that EVG is mostly allocated to securities with strong credit ratings. For reference, borrowers with a credit rating of BBB and above are classified as investment grade. These are typically the highest-quality borrowers. The breakdown by investment grade is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 48.8%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 46.6%
  • Not Rated: 4.7%

Most importantly, investors need to limit their expectations around EVG's ability to provide attractive growth over time. The main objective is income generation, and the fund doesn't have exposure to traditional equities, which means that EVG may miss out on market rallies. For instance, EVG's share price has declined by about 18.76% over the last decade. When including all distributions paid, the total return jumps up to 80.39% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from the fund's ability to continue paying out a high rate of distributions.

Valuation & Risk

Since EVG is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, EVG now trades at a discount to NAV of 4.4%. For reference, EVG has traded at an average discount to NAV of 3.1% over the last five-year period. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that EVG now trades at a fairly reasonable price-to-NAV level in relation to its historical range. This can be an indication that it's a solid time to accumulate.

To get better clarity into the internal performance of the fund, I reviewed the latest annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.66 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.44 per share. Looking back at its prior years, net investment income was mostly aligned with its typical range. The slight decrease in net investment income could just be a result of a lower interest rate environment compared to the last two years. Since net investment income doesn't support the distribution in its entirety, this leaves the fund reliant on net realized gains to bridge the gap in earnings.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions. This is why we see such large swings over the last few years, since EVG may be able to capture high gains during periods where market indexes are rising, which allows securities to be sold at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Despite the variable nature of net realized gains, EVG has demonstrated its ability to produce positive results on a consistent basis. This has ultimately led to positive NAV growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $13.31 per share
  • 2022: $10.48 per share
  • 2023: $10.49 per share
  • 2024: $11.18 per share
  • 2025: $11.38 per share

As part of the fund's operating strategy, EVG utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify its earnings. The use of leverage currently amounts to 26.7% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$40M. I consider this to be an aggressive use of leverage during this period of elevated interest rates. This is because the spread between the cost of EVG's debt and the yield collected from the securities is thinner, which makes leverage a lot less efficient. Additionally, investors need to consider the risk that leverage amplifies the downside risk if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated.

For instance, a sizeable portion of EVG's securities have a lower credit rating. Historically, this is associated with the risk of being impacted by borrower defaults. S&P Global estimates that the rate of leveraged loan defaults could increase as high as 3.5% in a scenario where interest rates remain high and slower economic growth occurs.

Despite the Fed initiating its third 25 basis point cut to interest rates, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. So the risk of defaults remains relevant and has caused EVG's price to have an inverse relationship over the last few years. So the hope is that future interest rate cuts will serve as a positive catalyst for growth and will be the driving force behind EVG's positive price momentum. The only challenge is that we don't know how long it'll take for interest rates to drop significantly.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0751 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.3%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.9012 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, we can see that EVG has consistently generated earnings that exceed the distributions being paid. For this reason, EVG remains a solid choice for investors seeking a reliable source of income generation. For reference, here are the year-end earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $1.13 per share
  • 2022: ($1.63) per share
  • 2023: $0.99 per share
  • 2024: $1.63 per share
  • 2025: $1.10 per share

Therefore, I have no concerns about the possibility of a dividend cut in the near term. As we can see below, EVG has maintained a very consistent payout over the last five years, with the exception being the decline that happened in 2022, which can be attributed to the market's decline that year. The fund's ability to consistently out-earn the distribution is a clear indication that the portfolio strategy is efficient through different market environments.

However, investors need to be aware that the income received from EVG may not offer any tax efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 81.6% of the distributions YTD were funded by net investment income. These distributions are classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in EVG may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax burden. On a positive note, the inclusion of some return of capital does lessen the tax implications, since this form of distribution isn't classified as income and therefore isn't taxed as such.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that EVG is attractively valued, and accumulation here may be rewarded in the future. The fund trades at a larger discount to NAV pricing than usual, which indicates a good buying opportunity. Assuming interest rates continue to trend downward over the next twelve months, EVG may see its share price regain some positive growth momentum as the operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities improves. Additionally, EVG has delivered strong dividend coverage over the last few years, and I believe that the fund remains a good choice for investors seeking a reliable stream of supplemental income generation.

data.title: Lithium Miners News For The Month Of December 2025

The past month saw lithium prices significantly higher again.

Lithium price news

Metal.com reported lithium spodumene concentrate Index (Li2O 5.5%-6.2%, excluding tax/insurance/freight) spot price of USD 1,378, as of December 22, 2025, significantly higher the past month.

The China lithium carbonate spot price was significantly higher last month, now at CNY 99,000. The CIF Asia lithium carbonate price is ~US$11.38/kg.

China lithium carbonate spot price 5 year chart - CNY 99,000 (~USD 14,060) (source)

Lithium demand versus supply outlook

BloombergNEF forecasts lithium deficits may begin in 2025 (as of Oct. 2024) (source)

RMIS forecasts lithium deficits to start after 2028, may grow very large without major new investments (as of May 2025) (source)

Albemarle is forecasting lithium deficits more likely from ~2027/28 (as of mid 2025) (source)

GGII, a Shenzhen-based consultancy, forecasts a balanced lithium market this year, a 10,000t LCE deficit in 2026, and a 60,000t deficit in 2027 (as of mid 2025)

Trend Investing v IEA demand forecast for EV metals (Trend Investing) (IEA)

data.title: Economic Growth In The U.S.: Doing Well
data.description:
The U.S. economy experienced an annualized 4.3 percent rate of growth in the third quarter of 2025.

This high of a rate of growth was not expected and represented the highest growth rate of the U.S. economy in two years. Note that this rate is a quarterly rate, annualized, to get a yearly rate.

The year-over-year rate of growth of real GDP came in at 2.3 percent.

This rate of growth fits right in with how the U.S. economy has been doing over the past 15 years or so.

Take a look at the long-term picture of U.S. economic growth.

Also, when one calculates the compound rate of economic growth from the summer of 2009 through the third quarter of 2025, one comes up with an economic growth figure of 2.4 percent.

In the summer of 2009, the Federal Reserve System, under the leadership of Chairman Ben Bernanke, established a "new" approach to monetary policy, a policy based upon the "quantitative" change in the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve.

This approach to monetary policy basically established a "supply side" growth pattern for the U.S. economy, and this supply-side growth model has dominated the Fed's results ever since. And the compound for the past 15 years includes the wide swings in the economy that are associated with the spread of the Covid-19 pandemic and the short economic recession that took place in 2020.

I have written many posts supporting this conclusion over the past year or so.

The result...the 2.4 percent compound rate of growth of real GDP going back to 2009.

The conclusion one can draw from this result is that the U.S. economy is continuing its "strong" performance generated by the steady monetary policy of the Federal Reserve System.

Note that the third-quarter data may still face substantial revisions because they have been assembled during a time when the U.S. statistical system was not open.

What about inflation?

Inflation, as measured by the PCE Index...the Personal Consumption Expenditure Index...rose at an annual rate of 2.9 percent in the third quarter of 2025. This is up from 2.6 percent in the second quarter...and is substantially higher than the 2.0 annual rate that the Federal Reserve has been shooting for.

It seems to be apparent that the U.S. economy is producing a much stronger performance than a lot of people expected.

As far as economic growth is concerned, economic growth certainly seems to be doing much better than the Trump administration thought it would be doing at this stage.

The question then has to be asked...do you think that there is any chance that the Trump administration will "back off" a little bit from the "aggressive" plan for economic stimulus it has promoted since the day it was elected?

My bet is that the Trump administration will not "back off" from pushing the aggressive stimulus program it has talked about...which includes getting a new, more aggressive Chairman of the Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System, a Chairman that will be a lot more likely to reduce the Federal Reserve policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate...by two or more percentage points.

Trump wants to push for faster economic growth.

His view...the way to get faster economic growth is to lower the Fed's policy rate of interest.

This effort will upset the "Bernanke" program, a program that has seemingly worked very well over the past fifteen years or so.

The program will spur on greater inflation.

And the program will disrupt the "supply side" approach to economic growth and will result in a more volatile economy.

This is my expectation.

We'll see.

One other point.

As my readers know, I much prefer to write about the year-over-year rate of growth of the real economy and not the "annualized" rate of growth of the real economy.

The quarterly numbers in the third quarter of 2025 show how the two approaches can give off substantially different pictures of how the economy is doing.

Which number represents the third quarter of 2025 the best?

The U.S. economy grew by an annualized 4.3 percent.

Or,

The U.S. economy grew by a year-over-year rate of growth of 2.3 percent.

The one number is almost twice the amount of the other number.

Which number gives the most appropriate picture of the existing situation?

I go with the year-over-year number in any case. To me, a year-over-year number gives me the most consistent picture of what the real situation is.

To me, the annualized number can be dominated by multiplying the actual "quarterly" number by 4 and providing a result that is a greater relative number than what is really achieved.

Saying that the U.S. economy grew by 4.3 percent in the third quarter of 2025 really makes it look like the economy is growing very rapidly.

Saying the U.S. economy grew by 2.3 percent in the third quarter of 2025 gives us a more realistic picture of what is going on in the economy and does not make it look like the economy is "taking off."

data.title: Allspring Utility And Telecommunications Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

  • The Utility & Telecommunications Fund increased 6.40% on a total return basis in the third quarter versus an increase of 7.57% for the S&P 500 Utilities Index benchmark.
  • Utilities holdings outperformed the benchmark.
  • Collectively, the fund's allocation to telecommunications and other sectors underperformed the benchmark.

Market review

Utilities' total return slightly lagged that of the S&P 500 Index during the quarter, and the sector remains neck and neck with the index year to date. Investors generally continued to favor riskier stocks, and the broader market made all-time highs despite meaningful geopolitical and economic concerns. As a result, several defensive sectors have lagged this year. However, utilities have performed well, driven by consistent and improving fundamentals, attractive valuations, and increased interest in companies that can produce power to supply data center expansion.

We believe the typically defensive utilities sector remains well positioned for whatever macroeconomic scenarios play out for the rest of the year. If the U.S. experiences an economic slowdown, we think regulated utilities' low volatility and steady, predictable earnings and dividend growth would be highly attractive to investors. Valuation looks attractive as well. The sector recently traded at approximately a 20% discount versus the broader market. We also continue to believe utilities are better positioned for growth than in recent memory. The U.S. looks like it has entered an era of rising electricity demand unseen in decades. Signs of demand growth emerged in 2023 and have since strengthened. Utilities across the country have raised growth forecasts. Beyond the much-discussed proliferation of data centers, utilities have also noted growing electric vehicle penetration and the reshoring of manufacturing to the U.S. as factors driving higher electricity usage. We believe more utilities could raise earnings guidance if demand growth further materializes.

Portfolio positioning Quarterly Attribution and Analysis

  • The fund's utility holdings outperformed the benchmark for the quarter, benefiting from not owning Consolidated Edison, Inc. (ED), and NRG Energy Inc. (NRG). ED operates in New York, which the managers view as a below-average regulatory environment for utilities, and we believe the company has below-average growth prospects. NRG shares, meanwhile, took a breather in the quarter, underperforming the index after significant outperformance in the first half. The other independent power producers (IPPs), a couple of which the fund does own, followed this pattern. The IPPs have all benefited from the theme of increasing demand for electricity. The largest overall contributor to relative performance was the position in Alphabet Inc. Class A (GOOGL), which rallied in our view due to a milder-than-expected antitrust ruling that avoided harsh remedies coupled with accelerating momentum in its artificial intelligence (AI) offerings, particularly in advertising and cloud services. Other telecommunications-related holdings detracted from performance in the quarter.
  • The fund invests a portion of the portfolio outside of the utilities and telecommunications sectors. These holdings overall underperformed the benchmark in the quarter. Financials holdings Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Inc. (MA) performed poorly on concerns around consumer spending trends and rising operating expenses. Health care holdings UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) and Amgen Inc. (AMGN) also underperformed the benchmark—in part, we think, due to the market's continued preference for riskier stocks. The managers sold the position in UNH after the company announced a CEO change, suspended financial guidance, and reportedly became the subject of a Department of Justice investigation into Medicare billing practices. We continue to believe that maintaining an allocation to non-utility and telecom stocks that offer what we expect to be solid, above-average dividend growth can benefit both overall portfolio dividend growth and total return over time.

Portfolio positioning - Outlook

  • The Inflation Reduction Act has created uncertainty for clean energy spending by rolling back certain tax incentives. While some projects may be delayed or canceled, we believe the ongoing transition to cleaner generation facilities is likely to continue. We think the demand growth many utilities are now forecasting may require an “all of the above” need for new generation capacity: gas, renewables, nuclear, and the delay of coal plant retirements. This should require robust spending on both new generation and expansion of the transmission and distribution grid. Even with a phasing out of tax credits, renewable generation seems well positioned given cost and speed-to-market advantages relative to natural gas and potential new nuclear facilities.
  • Increasing capital spending is an important challenge for the sector. While capital investments drive earnings and dividend growth for utilities, they also put pressure on customer bills. As a result, regulators could choose to limit incremental capital spending to mitigate bill increases. We do not think regulators will meaningfully constrain investment, however, as the majority target the safety and reliability of systems and accommodate the addition of new customers. Furthermore, we think most utilities have done an admirable job limiting expense growth to offset the effect of additional capital spending on customer bills.
  • Much of the telecommunications sector operates in mature, slow-growth markets, including television broadcasting and entertainment, internet access, wireless communications and infrastructure, and data networking. The managers do not expect the environment to change meaningfully in the near term, although longer term, emerging use cases in areas like 5G wireless technology, cloud computing, and AI could improve growth prospects. The managers have reduced exposure to these slower-growing markets. By adding positions in META and GOOGL shares, the fund has begun to tilt toward faster-growing communications applications.

Benchmark Descriptions:

The S&P 500 Utilities Index is a market-value-weighted index that measures the performance of all stocks within the utilities sector of the S&P 500 Index. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 stocks chosen for market size, liquidity, and industry group representation. It is a market-value-weighted index with each stock's weight in the index proportionate to its market value. You cannot invest directly in an index.

The Lipper averages are compiled by Lipper, Inc., an independent mutual fund research and rating service. Each Lipper average represents a universe of funds that are similar in investment objective. You cannot invest directly in a Lipper average.

The Morningstar Category average is the average return for the peer group based on the returns of each individual fund within the group. The total return of the Morningstar Category average does not include the effect of sales charges. You cannot invest directly in a Morningstar Category average.

Attribution Analysis:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

Definition of Terms:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, a downside capture ratio less than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and a downside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

R-squared is a measurement of how similar a fund's historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund's performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund's performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment's performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager's performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund's performance and a benchmark's performance.

Upside capture measures a fund's replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Risks:

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Stock values fluctuate in response to the activities of individual companies and general market and economic conditions. Funds that concentrate their investments in limited sectors, such as utilities and telecommunication services, are more vulnerable to adverse market, economic, regulatory, political, or other developments affecting those sectors. Non-diversified funds are more vulnerable to market or economic events impacting issuers of individual portfolio securities than a diversified fund. Consult the fund's prospectus for additional information on these and other risks.

The Morningstar Rating for funds, or star rating, is calculated for managed products (including mutual funds, variable annuity and variable life subaccounts, exchange-traded funds, closed-end funds, and separate accounts) with at least a three-year history. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. It is calculated based on a Morningstar risk-adjusted return measure that accounts for variation in a managed product's monthly excess performance, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The Morningstar Rating does not include any adjustment for sales loads. The top 10% of products in each product category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars, and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The Overall Morningstar Rating for a managed product is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5-, and 10-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36–59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60–119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods.

Across U.S.-domiciled utilities funds, the Utility and Telecommunications Fund received 2 stars among 57 funds, 2 stars among 56 funds, and 3 stars among 50 funds for the 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods, respectively. The Morningstar Rating is for the Institutional Class only; other classes may have different performance characteristics.
